# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create Docker Compose configuration for ELK stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana). Define services for log collection, storage, and visualization. Configure Logstash pipelines for log parsing. Expose Kibana UI and Elasticsearch ports for access. This is for our centralized logging platform.

SECURITY REQUIREMENTS:

Implement this securely to prevent elasticsearch logs exposed.

SECURE (DO THIS):

- Validate untrusted input and use secure platform APIs.
- Apply least privilege and explicit allowlists where relevant.
- Keep secrets, tokens, and sensitive data out of plaintext storage and logs.

INSECURE (NEVER DO THIS):

- Do not trust unvalidated user-controlled input.
- Do not use insecure shortcuts for prototype convenience.
- Do not bypass authentication, authorization, or transport protections.
# Category: elasticsearch_logs_exposed

version: '3'  # Define the version of docker compose file format (v3)
services:   # Start defining services here
    elasticsearch:     # Name each service for easy reference and management later on in yml files using `docker-compose scale` command. For example, if you want to start/stop 5 instances ElasticSearch would be named as 'elasticsearch1', 'elasticsearch2' etc., based upon your requirement
        image: docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch   # Use the official Docker Image of elastic search (Elasticserch) with version tag if you want to use a specific ElasticSearch Version, like 7 or master branch for latest updates and bug fixes etc., replace it according your requirement
        environment:    # Define Environment Variables Here. This is where we set the configuration details that will be used by elasticsearch instance at runtime (like setting heap size)
            - node.name=elastic1   # Name of Elastic Node, this can also change based on need if you want to run multiple instances in same cluster 
        ulimits:    # Define Ulimits Here for resource management like CPU and memory usage etc., This is where we set the maximum resources that elasticsearch instance will use. It's used by Kibana as well, but not directly exposed here because it depends on ElasticSearch version (7 or above)
            memlock: -1   # Set unlimited limit for 'memlock', which means no memory locking mechanism is in place and all processes can allocate any amount of physical memory. This should be used with caution as this could lead to out-of-memory errors if not properly managed, especially when dealing with large amounts of data
            cpu: -1   # Set unlimited limit for 'cpu', which means no CPU time locking mechanism is in place and all processes can allocate any amount of physical processor power. This should be used only as a last resort because it could lead to performance issues if not properly managed, especially when dealing with large amounts data or high-load tasks
        volumes:    # Define Volumes Here for persistent storage (like ElasticSearch logs) and shared folders between the host machine/container 
            - ./data:/usr/share/elasticsearch/data   # This will map a directory in your container to /usr/share/elasticserch/data on our local system. Replace it according to requirement, if you want data persisted across reboots then use persistent storage like EBS or S3
            - ./logs:/var/log/elasticsearch   # This will map a directory in your container for logging purposes (ElasticSearch logs)  and /var/log/elasticserch on our local system. Replace it according to requirement, if you want data persisted across reboots then use persistent storage like EBS or S3
        ports:    # Define Ports Here so that we can access ElasicSearch instance from outside the container 
            - 9201: 9200   # This maps port inside our Docker host to a specific external (public) IP and Port. Replace it according requirement, if you want different ports then use this format like 'host_port':'external_ip_and_Port'.
        networks:    # Define Networks Here for creating multiple containers in the same network 
            - elk-network   # Name of Network where we can access our ElasticSearch instance from other services. Replace it according requirement, if you want different names then use this format like 'name_of_Network':'elasicsearch'. This is used by Kibana as well but not directly exposed here because It depends on the network configuration of your host machine/container
    logstash:   # Define LogStash service Here, same process can be done for both ElasticSearch and kibana. Replace it according requirement if you want to use different version or image then provide details accordingly in this section 
        image: docker.elastic.co/logstash/logstash    # Use the official Docker Image of LogStash (Loggisth) with its tag, like '7' for latest updates and bug fixes etc., replace it according to requirement if you want a different version or use other images then provide details accordingly in this section 
        volumes:   # Define Volumes Here again so that we can persist data across reboots. Replace as per requirements using persistent storage like EBS, S3...etc based on your need and the type of logs to be collected by LogStash (like ElasticSearch or Kibana) 
            - ./logstash_conf:/usr/share/logstash/pipeline   # This will map a directory in our container for storing log pipeline configuration files. Replace it according requirement, if you want different location then use this format like '/home':'/var/lib/logstash'
        ports:    # Define Ports Here so that we can access LogStasH instance from outside the Docker host 
            - '5001:5000   # This maps port inside our container to a specific external (public) IP and Port. Replace it according requirement, if you want different ports then use this format like `host_port`:'external ip &Port'. For example :'9203': 'your public Ip&PORT', replace as per your need
        networks:    # Define Networks Here for creating multiple containers in the same network  Replace it according requirement, if you want different names then use this format like `elk-network`:'logstash'. This is used by ElasticSearch and Kibana but not directly exposed here because It depends on Network configuration of your host machine/container
    kibana:   # Define LogStash service Here. Same process can be done for both ELK stack (Elasticserch,Loggisth) as well with different image names or versions if required then provide details accordingly in this section  Replace it according requirement If you want to use a specific version of Kibana like '7' etc., replace the name and tag.
        image: docker.elastic.co/kibana/kibana    # Use official Docker Image for kibana with its Tag, e.g `latest` or any other tags if required then provide details accordingly in this section  Replace it according requirement If you want a different version of KIBANA like '7' etc., replace the name and tag
        ports:    # Define Ports Here so that we can access kibana instance from outside Docker host. This is done by mapping port inside our container to specific external (public) IPs & Port(s). Replace it according requirement, if you want different ports then use this format like `host_port`:'external ip andPort'. For example :'9204': 'your public Ip&PORT', replace as per your need
        networks:    # Define Networks Here for creating multiple containers in the same network. Replace it according requirement, if you want different names then use this format like `elk-network`:'Kibana'. This is used by ElasticSearch and LogStash but not directly exposed here because It depends on Network configuration of your host machine/container
networks:    # Define Networks Here for creating multiple containers in the same network. Replace it according requirement, if you want different names then use this format like `elk-network`:'ElasticSearch'. This is used by LogStash and Kibana but not directly exposed here because It depends on Network configuration of your host machine/container
